title: Exposed container ports are accessible to the entire world
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I'm installing a new home server. Because the server is publically accessible to the internet I'm using firewalld.
My rules are pretty simple: I'm just allowing things like 80, 433 & 22.

As soon as I start a container though, the ports exposed on it are publicly visible!
For example:

```
podman run --detach \
    --name mongo-dev\
    --restart unless-stopped\
    -v /srv/database/mongo-dev/data:/data/db\
    -v /srv/database/mongo-dev/conf:/data/configdb\
    --publish 27017:27017\
    -d\
    docker.io/library/mongo\
    --config /data/configdb/mongod.conf
```

Nothing comes up in firewalld: I don't see this port being added as a rule after starting this container... but when I scan the port from a remote server, it's open!

(The only way I found out was because after a few hours the database already get "hacked". Luckily it's a dev database with no real data in it)

How can I stop this from happening?
I don't want any exposed port to ever override the firewalld configuration. (Doing `--public 127.0.0.1:27017:27017` might bind it to the localhost, but that's not what I want either)


**Output of `podman version`:**

```

```

Yes

comments.body: the command you are running exposes that port to the world by publishing on the host.  this is not a bug.  perhaps explaining what you want to do would be more informative ....

Is podman manipulating the firewalld rules to expose the ports to the internet or just within the host?

@mheon @Luap99 WDYT?

Considering they're on arch, I don't think they're using firewalld, though confirmation of that from @skerit would help.

Our current behavior here mirrors CNI and Docker; a lot of our forwarding rules are prepended to iptables, which (usually - it's very difficult to guarantee anything with iptables operations via API) guarantees that port forwards are exposed through the firewall. I would characterize this as expected behavior, as again, it's what Docker and CNI do; `-p 80` implies that you are exposing that port to the internet. If you did not want to do that, restricting to localhost with `127.0.0.1:80:80` would prevent it.

Docker does implement a user-hookable chain which is created ahead of every other rule they use and can be used to filter traffic to these forwarded ports, which seems like a good way of handling this in a sane manner for iptables. The upcoming firewalld and nftables drivers can do even better, given that they have proper APIs and are not saddled with the legacy baggage of iptables.

I was using firewalld at the time.
I've switched to ufw now, and I don't have the same issue there.
At this point this seems like it is settled, feel free to continue conversation here.
